Users of lotion, cosmetics, cologne or perfume, nail polish, sun lotion, or hair gel had 28 to 80 percent higher total molar phthalate-plus-paraben concentrations (PMC4016195, sources/markdown/PMC4016195.md#BioC-passage-0032). One hundred Latina adolescents replaced their usual personal-care products for three days with products labeled free of the target chemicals and provided paired pre-intervention and post-intervention urine samples (PMC5047791, sources/markdown/PMC5047791.md#BioC-passage-0005). Benzene was detected in 83 percent of the tested feminine-hygiene products and reached 3,604 nanograms per gram in one spray (PMC7958867, sources/markdown/PMC7958867.md#BioC-passage-0034). Black participants had a lower mean gestational age than White participants, but the contrast's confidence interval crossed zero and only 8 percent of the 154-person cohort was Black (PMC8316883, sources/markdown/PMC8316883.md#BioC-passage-0040). This review synthesizes endocrine evidence for benzophenones, camphor derivatives, and cinnamate ultraviolet filters rather than reporting a new human-exposure study (PMC4997468, sources/markdown/PMC4997468.md#BioC-passage-0002). Participants with higher urinary MECPP reported using more total products, with weaker evidence for exposure to more ingredients of concern (PMC11764522, sources/markdown/PMC11764522.md#BioC-passage-0037). Toluene and d-limonene had the highest median volatile-organic-compound concentrations in the sampled nail salons (PMC9566193, sources/markdown/PMC9566193.md#BioC-passage-0026). Twenty nail salons were monitored for about 72 hours with volatile-organic-compound badges, and phthalates were assessed with silicone wristbands in ten salons (PMC9566193, sources/markdown/PMC9566193.md#BioC-passage-0013).
